checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains headings and paragraphs that correspond directly to the source PDF. No fabricated content or invented section descriptions were found. The metadata title is an expected AI-generated addition.

All contact information present in the output (specifically the URL at the end of the document) exists in the source PDF. No fabricated phone numbers, emails, or URLs were introduced. While some URLs are missing from the output, no new ones were created.

The output includes content from all 10 pages of the source PDF. All major sections (Present, Proceedings, Priorities 1-3, GIS Study, Presentation, Data Sharing Committee, Committee Reports, etc.) are represented.
The source document contains no formal tables; the information is presented in text, headings, and lists. The lists (recommendations) in the output match the row/item counts in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The sections are largely intact. Although two URLs are omitted, this does not constitute a loss of more than 25% of any section's text content. The paragraphs surrounding the omissions are complete.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: There are two instances where crucial URLs were omitted from the output. In Priority #2 (Page 2), the URL 'www.ncstreams.net' is missing between reading_order 15 and 17. In the Orthoimagery Planning section (Page 7), the URL 'www.nconemap.net' is missing between reading_order 60 and 62. These omissions alter the informational utility of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: P2: 'available on the project website at www.ncstreams.net .'; P7: 'is on the NC OneMap website, www.nconemap.net. Mr. Nagy'
checks.b_evidence_output: P2: 'available on the project website at ' (reading_order 15), '. The Upper French Broad' (reading_order 17); P7: 'is on the NC OneMap website, ' (reading_order 60), '. Mr. Nagy said' (reading_order 62)
checks.b_evidence_location: Priority #2 (Page 2, element 15) and Orthoimagery Planning (Page 7, element 60)

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain any duplicated text blocks. Footers and headers that appear on multiple pages of the source document (e.g., 'GICC Minutes, August 8, 2007—X') have been correctly omitted from the semantic output rather than duplicated.

The heading hierarchy is significantly corrupted in the latter half of the document. Elements 74, 75, and 76 (Federal Interagency Committee, GIS TAC, and Management and Operations Committee) are tagged as Level 2 headings, but they contain the entire paragraph text for those sections rather than just a title. Furthermore, as these are sub-sections of 'Committee Reports' (also H2, element 54), assigning them Level 2 is a hierarchy error. Promoting full paragraphs to H2 headers breaks the logical structure and navigation for screen readers.
checks.b_evidence_source: Federal Interagency Committee (FIC). Mary Combs reported on the NAIP photography (leaf-on) options for 2008 and the fact that North Carolina will not be flown in 2007. The FIC will continue to discuss land use planning around military installations in North Carolina as directed by the GICC.
checks.b_evidence_output:
{
  "type": "heading",
  "level": 2,
  "text": "Federal Interagency Committee (FIC). Mary Combs reported on the NAIP photography (leaf-on) options for 2008 and the fact that North Carolina will not be flown in 2007. The FIC will continue to discuss land use planning around military installations in North Carolina as directed by the GICC.",
  "id": null,
  "lang": null,
  "reading_order": 74
}

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no table structure corruption exists in the output.

The overall reading order of the document is logical and matches the source from page 1 through 10. While some minor text loss occurs at URL boundaries, the sequence of sections and paragraphs is correct.

Lists are generally identified correctly. The list of nine recommendations on pages 4 and 5 is split into two separate list elements in the JSON (elements 40 and 42) due to the page break, but the structure of the list items is preserved. Action items are handled inconsistently (some as lists, some as paragraphs), but this does not materially corrupt the document's meaning.

Two critical URLs present in the source document are missing from the output text. In the section for 'Priority #2', the URL 'www.ncstreams.net' is missing where it should appear at the end of a sentence. Similarly, in the 'Orthoimagery Planning' section, the URL 'www.nconemap.net' is missing. These appear to have been dropped during the conversion process, likely due to line breaks or trailing punctuation.
checks.b_evidence_source: available on the project website at www.ncstreams.net . The Upper French Broad... [Page 2]; ...website, www.nconemap.net. Mr. Nagy said... [Page 7]
checks.b_evidence_output: ...available on the project website at [MISSING] . The Upper French Broad... [Element 15/17]; ...website, [MISSING]. Mr. Nagy said... [Element 60/62]

checks.a_analysis: The HTML rendering faithfully preserves the single-column visual organization of the source PDF. The document's structure—comprising the header, member attendance, proceedings, specific project priorities, committee reports, and adjournment—is presented in a logical linear flow that matches the source's intent. The transition from fixed pages to a responsive web layout does not disrupt the visual organization of the information.

The source document consists entirely of text paragraphs, headings, and numbered lists. No actual tables (grid-based data layouts) are present in the original PDF, so there are no instances of broken or misrendered tables in the output.

The source PDF is a text-only document. There are no meaningful images, such as agency logos, seal, charts, or maps, present in the original material. The absence of images in the HTML output is therefore accurate.

No visual duplication of body content blocks, such as paragraphs or headings, was identified in the HTML rendering. The Table of Contents sidebar provides navigation links to the document sections, which is an expected accessibility feature and not a content duplication defect. The page-specific header/footer text (e.g., 'GICC Minutes, August 8, 2007—1') appears correctly as markers for the linearized sections.
